request.question: How many venues are involved in the main tournament which is part of the English Cricket Team's 2017-2018 schedule?

title: 2017–18 Ashes series
Betty Archdale
Centenary Test
Bob Wyatt
2005 English cricket season
English cricket team in India in 2005–06

English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18
sentences: The 2017–18 Ashes series is a forthcoming series of Test cricket matches to be contested between England and Australia for The Ashes.
The series will be played at five venues across Australia between 23 November 2017 and 8 January 2018.
Helen Elizabeth "Betty" Archdale (21 August 1907 – 11 January 2000) was an educationalist and cricketer.
She was a captain of the English women's cricket team in 1934 and 1935.
In 1934/35 she led the first English cricket team to tour Australia and New Zealand, the result of which was a 2-0 victory over Australia.
This tour did much both to raise the status of women's cricket and to heal some of the damage done to Anglo-Australian cricket relations by bodyline two years earlier.
Centenary Test refers to two matches of Test cricket played between the English cricket team and the Australian cricket team, the first in 1977 and the second in 1980.
These matches were played to mark the 100th anniversaries of the first Test cricket matches played in Australia (1877) and in England (1880) respectively.
Neither match was played for The Ashes.
Robert "Bob" Elliott Storey Wyatt (2 May 1901, Milford Heath House, Surrey, England – 20 April 1995, Treliske, Truro, Cornwall) was an English cricket player.
He played for Warwickshire, Worcestershire, and the English cricket team.
Before the beginning of the 2005 English cricket season began, a resurgent English cricket team had won four Test series in a row, going unbeaten through the 2004 calendar year.
The start of the international season saw England defeat Bangladesh 2–0 in their two-match series, winning both Tests by an innings.
This was followed by a tri-nations one-day tournament that also featured Australia.
However, all eyes were on the Ashes.
Australia still started as favourites, but England, for the first time in years, were expected to put up a real challenge to Aussie supremacy.
The English cricket team toured India during February, March and April 2006.

The English cricket team was aspiring to maintain the form that took them to second place in the ICC Test Championship before their disastrous spell against Pakistan, and which helped win the 2005 Ashes series at home to Australia.

This goal was substantially hindered by the usual stomach complaints which nearly always dog the English team in the opening weeks of Indian tours, and a recurrence of an injury to the captain Michael Vaughan; the swing bowler Simon Jones and the absence of Ashley Giles who missed the tour for an operation.

As well as this, stand-in captain Marcus Trescothick flew home for "personal reasons", not wishing to divulge further, leaving Andrew Flintoff, who missed the birth of his son, to take on the title of skipper for the first time having to captain two maiden international cricketers on the English side: Alastair Cook and Monty Panesar as well as Shanthakumaran Sreesanth, Piyush Chawla and Munaf Patel for the home team.

International cricket played in the 2006 cricket season is defined as matches scheduled between May and August 2006 in all cricketing countries, as well as all international matches scheduled for the 2006 English cricket season.
Matches between January and April are defined as belonging to the 2005–06 season, while matches between September and December are defined as the 2006–07 season.

The main matches in this period were played in England, as this was in the middle of the English cricket season, but the third edition of the ICC Intercontinental Cup is defined as a part of the 2006 season, even though the tournament will stretch into February 2007, and three A teams are also scheduled to tour the northwestern part of Australia in June and July 2006.
The Barmy Army is a semi-organised group of English cricket fans which arranges touring parties for some of its members to follow the English cricket team on overseas tours.
The name is also applied to followers of the team who join in with match day activities in the crowd, but do not necessarily travel as part of an organised tour.
The 2004 English cricket season saw the English cricket team record a 4-0 Test series whitewash over the West Indies and a comfortable 3-0 win over New Zealand.
Their one-day form was sporadic, however.
In the Natwest Trophy, they failed to make the final, which saw New Zealand defeat the West Indies by 107 runs.
In the Natwest Challenge, they beat India 2-1.
In domestic cricket, Warwickshire won the County Championship.
The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).
They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.
The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.
In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.

title: English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18
2017–18 Ashes series

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: How many venues are involved in the main tournament which is part of the English Cricket Team's 2017-2018 schedule?
hop1_docs: English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18 | The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).  They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.  The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.  In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.

2017–18 Regional Super50 | The 2017–18 Regional Super50 is scheduled to be the 44th edition of the Regional Super50, the domestic limited-overs cricket competition for the countries of the West Indies Cricket Board (WICB).  The tournament is scheduled to be held in early 2018, with the English county sides Kent and Hampshire being invited to take part in the competition.  Kent took part in the previous edition of the tournament.  Barbados are the defending champions.

2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Four | The 2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Four is an international cricket tournament that is scheduled to take place during mid-2018.  It will form part of the 2017–22 cycle of the World Cricket League (WCL) which determines the qualification for the 2023 Cricket World Cup.

2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Two | 2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Two is a cricket tournament that is scheduled to take place in 2018.  Oman and Canada were the top two teams in the 2017 ICC World Cricket League Division Three tournament in Uganda and were promoted as a result.  They will be joined by the bottom four teams from the ongoing 2015–17 ICC World Cricket League Championship to determine the final two spots in the 2018 Cricket World Cup Qualifier.

2017–18 Pac-12 Conference men's basketball season | The 2017–18 Pac-12 Conference men's basketball season begins with practices in October 2017 followed by the 2017–18 NCAA Division I men's basketball season in November 10, 2017 with the conference schedule will begin on December 27, 2017.  The 2018 Pac-12 Tournament in March 2018 at the T-Mobile Arena in Paradise, Nevada.  Due to the Pac-12's use of that venue for their conference tournament, the Pac-12 Tournament will take place one week earlier than usual, ending the week before Selection Sunday.  This could result in teams having nearly two weeks off before the 2018 NCAA tournament.  As a result, it is anticipated that the Pac-12 regular season will begin in late-December.  Pac-12 teams' first Pac-12 game would likely be on the weekend of December, 2017 with a second game on December , 2017.  Coaches have requested that Pac-12 game be scheduled between Christmas and New Year's Day, accordingly each team will play two conference games in early December before finishing non-conference play.

2017–18 Pro50 Championship | The 2017–18 Pro50 Championship is scheduled to be the fifteenth edition of the Pro50 Championship, a List A cricket tournament in Zimbabwe.  The competition will run from December 2017 to March 2018 and will include a new team, the Rising Stars, along with the existing four teams.  Matabeleland Tuskers are the defending champions.

2018 ICC Women's World Twenty20 | The 2018 ICC Women's World Twenty20 will be hosted in the West Indies from 2 to 25 November 2018, during the 2018–19 international cricket season.  It will be the sixth edition of the ICC Women's World Twenty20, and the second hosted by the West Indies (after the 2010 edition).  The tournament was awarded to the West Indies Cricket Board (WICB) at the 2013 annual conference of the International Cricket Council (ICC).  The tournament's dates were confirmed at an ICC board meeting in January 2015.  In February 2017, the ICC confirmed that this would be the first T20 tournament that uses the Umpire Decision Review System, with one review per side.  The qualifier tournament for the competition is scheduled to be held in late 2017 or early 2018.

response.summary_1:
Facts from supplied passages:
- [1] "scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018" (England tour of Australia 2017–18).
- [1] "to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs)."
- [1] "They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament" (tri‑series) and "New Zealand, who will co‑host the tournament along with Australia."
- [1] "The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series."
- [1] "WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth."
- [2] The "2017–18 Regional Super50" exists and involves invited English county sides (Kent and Hampshire) — but this is a domestic West Indies tournament, not clearly the England mainschedule item.
- [3–7] Other tournament pages listed but none give venue counts for England's 2017–18 main tournament.

Bridge entities / ambiguities blocking an answer:
- "main tournament" is ambiguous in context: candidate interpretations from passages are
  - Candidate A: "2017–18 Ashes series" (mentioned in [1]) — ambiguous.
  - Candidate B: "three‑nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament / tri‑series" (mentioned in [1]) — ambiguous.
  - Candidate C: less likely: "Regional Super50" or other tournaments in passages [2–7] (not shown as England's main tournament).
- Missing/ambiguous facts: none of the passages provide the number of venues for any of the candidate tournaments; also the source does not label which of these is the "main tournament."

Prioritized next‑hop retrievals to resolve ambiguity and obtain the venue count:
1) Fetch the full "English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18" page (the tour page referenced in [1]) — extract the infobox or any section/phrase containing "Main tournament", "Part of", or explicit statement identifying which tournament is the tour's main tournament. This will resolve which candidate (Ashes vs tri‑series) is meant.
2) If the main tournament is the Ashes: fetch the "2017–18 Ashes series" (or "The Ashes 2017–18") page — extract the "Venues" or "Grounds" section or the match list (count unique venues/grounds).
3) If the main tournament is the tri‑series: fetch the specific tri‑series page (the 2017–18 three‑nation T20I tri‑series page; possible titles: "2017–18 Trans‑Tasman Tri‑Series" or tri‑series including England/Australia/New Zealand) — extract the "Venues", "Hosts" or "Matches" section and count unique venues.
4) If neither page exists in results, fetch both tournament pages regardless (Ashes and tri‑series) and extract venue lists or the schedule/fixtures section (keywords: "Venues", "Grounds", "Stadiums", "Matches", "Fixtures", "Host cities") to compute the number.

No final answer is given here because the supplied passages do not unambiguously identify the "main tournament" nor provide any venue counts.
response.hop2_query: England tour of Australia 2017–18 identify "main tournament"
hop2_docs: 2017–18 Trans-Tasman Tri-Series | The 2017–18 Trans-Tasman Tri-Series is an upcoming Twenty20 International (T20I) cricket tournament scheduled to be held in Australia and New Zealand in February 2018.  It is a tri-nation series between Australia, England and New Zealand.  It follows on from England's tour of Australia, which includes the 2017–18 Ashes series, and takes the place of the planned Chappell–Hadlee Trophy series.

English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18 | The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).  They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.  The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.  In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.

2017 PGA Tour of Australasia | The 2017 PGA Tour of Australasia will be a series of men's professional golf events played mainly in Australia.  The main tournaments on the PGA Tour of Australasia are played in the southern summer, so they are split between the first and last months of the year.

2017 Brisbane International | The 2017 Brisbane International was a tournament of the 2017 ATP World Tour and 2017 WTA Tour.  It was played on outdoor hard courts in Brisbane, Queensland, Australia.  It was the ninth edition of the tournament and took place at the Queensland Tennis Centre in Tennyson.  It was held from 1 to 8 January 2017 as part of the Australian Open Series in preparation for the first Grand Slam of the year.

2017 Ricoh Open | The 2017 Ricoh Open was a tennis tournament played on outdoor grass courts.  It was the 28th edition of the Rosmalen Grass Court Championships, and part of the 250 Series of the 2017 ATP World Tour, and of the WTA International tournaments of the 2017 WTA Tour.  Both the men's and the women's events took place at the Autotron park in Rosmalen, Rosmalen, Netherlands, from June 12 through June 18, 2017.

2017 Aegon Open Nottingham | The 2017 Aegon Open Nottingham was a professional tennis tournament played on outdoor grass courts.  It was the 10th edition of the event for women and the 22nd edition for men, with the men's event being downgraded from an ATP World Tour 250 series event to an ATP Challenger Tour event in 2017.  It was classified as a WTA International tournament on the 2017 WTA Tour.  The event took place at the Nottingham Tennis Centre in Nottingham, United Kingdom from 12 June through 18 June 2017.

2017 Canberra Challenger | The 2017 Canberra Challenger is a professional tennis tournament played on outdoor hard courts.  It is the second edition of the tournament, which is a part of the 2017 ATP Challenger Tour.  It takes place in Canberra, Australia between 9 and 14 January 2017.

response.summary_2: The candidate main tournaments are the 2017–18 Ashes series and the 2017–18 Trans‑Tasman Tri‑Series. The 2017–18 Ashes series is the Test series that forms part of England’s tour of Australia in 2017–18. The 2017–18 Trans‑Tasman Tri‑Series is a three‑nation T20I tournament between Australia, England and New Zealand, scheduled to be held in Australia and New Zealand in February 2018.
